content.text: Artifacts are a special feature in the Claude.ai web interface that allows Claude to produce substantial, standalone content — code files, documents, websites, data visualizations, and more — in a dedicated side panel separate from the main conversation. When Claude creates an artifact, it appears in its own panel where you can view it, edit it, copy it, and iterate on it without cluttering the conversation thread. Artifacts are ideal for any content that has value as a standalone deliverable rather than just part of a chat exchange.
What Are Artifacts in Claude

When you ask Claude to write a substantial piece of content — a Python script, an HTML webpage, a markdown document, a SQL query, or a formatted report — Claude can render that content as an artifact rather than embedding it inline in the conversation. The artifact appears in a panel on the right side of the Claude.ai interface, where it is rendered appropriately for its type. HTML artifacts are rendered as live web pages. Code artifacts are displayed with syntax highlighting. Markdown artifacts are rendered with formatting applied.

Artifacts are 'owned' by the conversation — they persist as long as the conversation exists and can be referenced and updated throughout the session. Each artifact has its own panel state, allowing you to switch between viewing the conversation and previewing the artifact. You can ask Claude to make changes to an existing artifact, and Claude will update it in place, maintaining a coherent working document rather than producing a new version in every reply.

The artifact system is designed for workflows where you are iteratively building something — a web app, a data analysis script, a business document — and want to refine it over multiple conversational turns. Rather than scrolling through a long chat history to find the latest version of your file, the artifact panel always shows the current state of what you are building.
Why Creating Artifacts Matters

Before the artifacts feature, working with Claude on substantial content meant copying code or text from chat messages, pasting it into your editor, asking for revisions, copying again, and repeating. This process was tedious and error-prone. Artifacts solve this by keeping the content in a dedicated, always-visible panel that Claude can update in place. The workflow becomes more like collaborative document editing and less like copying and pasting from a chat.
Persistent working space: Artifacts give your content a dedicated home in the conversation, always visible and always up to date.
Live preview for HTML and other rendered formats: You can see exactly how an HTML artifact looks in a browser without having to copy and paste it elsewhere.
Easy iteration: Ask Claude to revise the artifact, and it updates the panel — no searching through chat history for the latest version.
One-click copying: A copy button in the artifact panel lets you grab the content quickly without manually selecting text.
Focused conversation: By moving code or documents to the artifact panel, the conversation thread stays clean and focused on instructions and discussion.
Download support: Some artifact types can be downloaded directly from the panel as files.
Types of Artifacts You Can Create
Code artifacts: Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, CSS, SQL, Bash, and many other programming languages, displayed with syntax highlighting.
HTML artifacts: Full HTML documents that render as live interactive web pages in the preview panel — great for prototypes, dashboards, and landing pages.
React artifacts: React component code that is rendered live in the panel, allowing you to preview interactive UI components.
Markdown artifacts: Formatted documents like reports, READMEs, or essays, rendered with full markdown formatting.
SVG artifacts: Scalable vector graphics rendered as visual images, useful for logos, diagrams, and illustrations.
Plain text artifacts: Unformatted text content like configuration files, CSV data, or plain prose that benefits from a dedicated display area.
Mermaid diagrams: Architecture diagrams, flowcharts, and sequence diagrams that Claude can generate using Mermaid syntax and render visually.
How to Create an Artifact

Creating an artifact is as simple as asking Claude to produce content that is substantial enough to warrant its own panel. Claude automatically decides whether content should be an artifact based on its type and length. Short code snippets in a conversational context are usually displayed inline. Longer, standalone pieces of content — a complete web page, a full Python script, a multi-page document — are typically rendered as artifacts.

You can also explicitly request an artifact by framing your request as asking for a standalone piece of content. Phrases like 'write a complete HTML page', 'create a Python script that...', or 'draft a report document' are signals to Claude that an artifact is appropriate. If Claude produces inline content and you want it as an artifact, you can ask 'can you put that in an artifact?' and Claude will reformulate the response accordingly.

Once the artifact appears in the panel, it is live and interactive. For HTML artifacts, you can click buttons, fill out forms, and interact with the page as you would in a browser. For code artifacts, the panel shows the syntax-highlighted source. For markdown, it shows the rendered document. The panel has controls to switch between the source view and the rendered preview depending on the artifact type.
Step-by-Step: Creating Your First Artifact

Open Claude.ai: Navigate to claude.ai in your web browser and sign in to your account. Artifacts are available on both the free and paid plans, though some artifact types may have limitations on the free tier.
Start a new conversation: Click 'New Chat' to open a fresh conversation. You can also create artifacts in an existing conversation if you want to continue working in context.

Write a prompt that requests substantial content: For example, 'Create a complete HTML page with a dark-themed to-do list app that uses local storage to save tasks'. Be specific about what you want so Claude can produce a well-defined artifact.
Submit your prompt: Press Enter or click the send button. Claude will process your request and determine whether to produce an artifact.

Watch the artifact panel open: If Claude determines that an artifact is appropriate, a panel will appear on the right side of the screen showing the content. For HTML, you will see the rendered page immediately.
Interact with and inspect the artifact: For HTML artifacts, click elements, test functionality, and see how it looks. For code, review the syntax. For documents, read the formatted output.

Iterate by asking for changes: Type follow-up instructions like 'make the background darker', 'add a delete button for each task', or 'add error handling to the Python function'. Claude will update the artifact in place.
Practical Examples

A small business owner wants a simple website for their bakery. They ask Claude to 'create a complete HTML page for a bakery called Sweet Crumbs — include a hero section, a menu with prices, an about us section, and a contact form'. Claude generates the full HTML, CSS, and JavaScript in an artifact. The owner can preview it immediately, ask for color changes, request new sections, or adjust the layout — all through conversation.

A data analyst needs a Python script to parse CSV files and generate charts. They ask Claude to 'write a Python script using pandas and matplotlib that reads a CSV file named sales_data.csv, groups the data by month, and produces a bar chart of monthly revenue'. Claude creates a code artifact with the complete script. The analyst can ask Claude to add error handling, support different chart types, or add command-line arguments — Claude updates the same artifact each time.

A technical writer drafts a user manual. They ask Claude to 'create a markdown document structured as a user guide for a task management app — include sections for getting started, creating tasks, managing projects, and keyboard shortcuts'. Claude produces a formatted markdown artifact with headings, bullet points, and tables. The writer iterates on each section, asking Claude to expand certain explanations, add examples, or adjust the tone.

A developer wants to prototype a dashboard. They ask Claude to 'create a React component for a sales dashboard that shows three KPI cards at the top and a bar chart below'. Claude creates a React artifact that renders live in the panel. The developer can see the UI immediately, request layout adjustments, add more metrics, or change the chart library — all without leaving the Claude.ai interface.
When to Use Artifacts
When creating content that you will use as a standalone file — a complete script, web page, or document — rather than just reading in the chat.
When you are iterating on a piece of content over multiple conversation turns and want to track the current state easily.
When the content benefits from live preview, such as HTML or React components where seeing the rendered output is important.
When you want clean separation between your instructions and Claude's deliverable output.
When you want to copy or download the output efficiently rather than selecting text from a chat message.
Limitations and Trade-offs
Browser-only: Artifacts are a Claude.ai web interface feature and are not available through the API or desktop app in the same form.
Session-scoped: Artifacts exist within a conversation and are not persistently stored outside of it — if the conversation is deleted, the artifact is gone.
No file system access: Artifacts cannot read from or write to your local file system — they are self-contained previews in the browser.
HTML sandbox: HTML artifacts run in a sandboxed iframe, which means they cannot access external URLs or local storage of your main browser session.
Complex apps may require copy-paste to test locally: For sophisticated applications with server-side components or file I/O, you will still need to copy the code and run it locally.
Tips and Best Practices
Ask for complete, standalone files rather than code snippets to trigger artifact creation consistently.
Be specific in your initial request — the more detail you give, the more complete and useful the initial artifact will be, reducing the number of revision rounds.
Use the 'source' view in the artifact panel to inspect the actual code even when viewing a rendered preview, so you can catch any issues before copying.
For long documents, ask Claude to create them section by section, then ask it to combine them into a final artifact — this prevents context limitations affecting quality.
When iterating, describe changes in terms of the outcome you want rather than specifying implementation details — Claude is good at translating goals into code changes.
Use the copy button in the artifact panel for clean copying — it avoids formatting issues that can occur when manually selecting rendered HTML or markdown.
For code artifacts that will be run locally, review the dependencies and imports before executing, ensuring you have the required libraries installed.
Combine artifacts with conversation: use the chat to discuss approach and trade-offs, then ask Claude to apply the agreed approach to the artifact.
Common Questions
How is an artifact different from code Claude writes in the chat?

When Claude writes code or a document directly in the chat, it appears as part of the conversation thread as a message. Artifacts appear in a separate panel on the right side of the interface. The key differences are that artifacts have a dedicated display area, support live rendering for HTML and React, can be updated in place across multiple turns, and have quick copy and download options. Artifacts are designed to be working documents that you refine over time, while inline chat content is more suitable for short explanations or snippets.
Can I save or export an artifact?

Yes. The artifact panel has a copy button that copies the content to your clipboard. Some artifact types also have a download button that lets you save the file directly. However, artifacts are not persistently stored in a Claude account library — they exist within the context of their conversation. If you close the conversation or the conversation is deleted, the artifact goes with it. For important work, always copy or download the artifact to your own file storage as soon as it is ready.
Can Claude create multiple artifacts in one conversation?

Yes. Claude can create multiple artifacts in a single conversation, and they each appear in the artifact panel. You can switch between them using controls in the panel. This is useful when building a project that has multiple components — for example, a frontend HTML file and a separate JavaScript file. Each artifact is tracked independently, and you can ask Claude to update any of them at any point in the conversation.

Overview

Creating & Editing Artifacts is a fundamental concept for anyone working with Claude and the Anthropic ecosystem. Understanding it deeply allows you to build more effective applications, write better prompts, and get more value from Claude's capabilities. This section provides a thorough exploration of the topic, covering both the theory and practical application aspects that you'll encounter in real-world usage.

Whether you're a beginner just starting out or an experienced developer looking to deepen your knowledge, this guide walks through everything you need to know about Creating & Editing Artifacts. We'll cover the core concepts, walk through concrete examples, address common questions, and show you how to apply what you've learned in practical scenarios.

Practical Examples
The best way to understand Creating & Editing Artifacts is through concrete examples. These examples range from simple to complex, illustrating different aspects of the topic.
Basic Example
The following example demonstrates the most fundamental use case for Creating & Editing Artifacts. This is a great starting point for understanding the core behavior.

# Example: Getting started with Creating & Editing Artifacts
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Basic usage example
message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Explain Creating & Editing Artifacts in simple terms with a practical example."
        }
    ]
)

print(message.content[0].text)
Advanced Example
This more advanced example shows how to use Creating & Editing Artifacts in a real-world scenario with proper error handling, configuration, and best practices applied.

# Advanced example: Production-ready Creating & Editing Artifacts implementation
import anthropic
from typing import Optional
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class ClaudeCreating&EditingartiHandler:
    """Handles Creating & Editing Artifacts with production best practices."""

    def __init__(self, api_key: Optional[str] = None):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=api_key)
        self.model = "claude-opus-4-5"

    def process(self, input_text: str, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> str:
        """Process input with error handling and logging."""
        try:
            logger.info(f"Processing request of length {len(input_text)}")
            response = self.client.messages.create(
                model=self.model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]
            )
            result = response.content[0].text
            logger.info(f"Successfully generated response of length {len(result)}")
            return result
        except anthropic.RateLimitError:
            logger.warning("Rate limit reached, consider reducing request frequency")
            raise
        except anthropic.APIError as e:
            logger.error(f"API error: {e}")
            raise

# Usage
handler = ClaudeCreating&EditingartiHandler()
result = handler.process("Your query here")
print(result)

Frequently Asked Questions
These are the most common questions about Creating & Editing Artifacts, based on developer feedback and support requests.
Q: What is the most important thing to understand about Creating & Editing Artifacts?

A: The most important thing to understand is that Creating & Editing Artifacts is a system that rewards careful, intentional usage. The more precisely you communicate your intent, the better results you'll get. Start with clear, specific inputs and refine based on the outputs you receive.
Q: How does this interact with other Claude features?

A: Creating & Editing Artifacts works together with most other Claude features. It's particularly important to understand how it interacts with system prompts, conversation history, and any tools or integrations you're using. In most cases, these interactions are synergistic rather than conflicting.
Q: What are the performance implications?

A: Performance depends on several factors: the complexity of your inputs, the length of your context window, and the Claude model you're using. Generally, simpler and more focused inputs produce faster and more accurate results. For high-throughput use cases, consider batching and caching strategies.
Q: How do I debug issues?

A: When debugging Creating & Editing Artifacts issues, start by isolating the problem. Simplify your input to the minimum that reproduces the issue, then gradually add complexity back. Check the API documentation for current behavior specifications and use logging to capture the full request-response cycle.
